title: Slab-built keepsake box in air-dry clay
subtitle: Four slab walls, a base, and a fitted lid — all joined with score-and-slip
excerpt: A keepsake box is the classic slab-building exercise: measuring and cutting four walls to equal height, joining them squarely at the corners, fitting a lid that sits inside a clay lip. This version uses air-dry clay and demonstrates the score-and-slip joining method.
sourceNotes: Technique derived from first principles. Slab box construction referenced from historical pottery manuals; score-and-slip is a foundational joining method documented in craft literature from the early twentieth century onward.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness, used as the building unit for box forms and flat panels
The technique of scratching cross-hatch marks into both clay surfaces and applying a thick clay-and-water paste before joining, to create a strong mechanical bond
A thick mixture of clay and water used as an adhesive to join separate clay pieces together
The stage at which clay holds its shape firmly but remains damp enough to carve and join without crumbling
Fully dried unfired clay; fragile and water-soluble until sealed

content.text: A
slab
the two most-used skills in hand-building: cutting flat pieces to measured dimensions and joining them with
score-and-slip
. The finished box (roughly 12 cm long, 9 cm wide, 6 cm tall) is sealed for use as a trinket or keepsake holder. No kiln is required. The lid fits inside a narrow clay lip so it does not slide.
Preparation
Condition the full 500 g of clay by pressing and folding until uniform. Set aside 80 g for the lid. Make a thick
slip

by mixing a walnut-sized lump of clay with a tablespoon of water until the texture is thick cream. You will use this for all joins. Set up slab guides at 6 mm. Cut a cardboard template: two rectangles 12 cm x 6 cm for the long walls, two rectangles 9 cm x 6 cm for the short walls, and one rectangle 12 cm x 9 cm for the base.
Rolling and cutting the slabs

Roll the clay in two batches to 6 mm thickness between the guides. Use your cardboard templates to cut out the five pieces: two long walls, two short walls, and one base. Hold the fettling knife vertical and draw it firmly along the template edge in one continuous stroke. Do not saw.

Smooth all cut edges immediately with a damp sponge. Place each piece flat on the board under a loose cloth to keep them at the same moisture level. Do not let any piece dry faster than the others, as mismatched moisture makes joining very difficult.
Roll the reserved 80 g of clay to 5 mm and cut the lid panel to 11.5 cm x 8.5 cm (0.5 cm smaller than the base on each side, so it will fit inside the lip).
Assembling the box

Begin with the base slab on the board. Using the scoring tool or needle tool, scratch cross-hatch marks about 5 mm wide along all four edges of the base where the walls will attach. Apply slip generously to the scored areas.

Score the bottom edge of one long wall and the corresponding base edge. Press the wall down firmly onto the base, wiping away excess slip. The wall stands at 90 degrees to the base. Hold it in place for 30 seconds, then lean it against a book or rolled cloth at the correct angle while you attach the opposite long wall.

Attach both short walls by the same method, scoring where they meet the base and the inner faces of the long walls. The short walls butt against the inside face of the long walls. Blend all interior corners with your finger in a continuous smooth stroke; this seals the join and reinforces the corner.
Check the box is square from above by measuring the two diagonals with a strip of paper. If they differ by more than 2 mm, gently press the longer diagonal's corners inward before the clay stiffens.
Adding the lid lip

From the remaining clay, roll and cut four strips 6 mm wide and 5 mm tall to form the inside lip that holds the lid in place. Score and slip the interior top edge of all four walls. Press the strips inside the top of the walls, standing them upright, set in about 3 mm from the outside edge. Blend the inner face of each strip down onto the wall. When the lid is placed inside, these strips will stop it sliding.
Drying and sealing
Cover the box loosely with a plastic bag and allow it to reach
leather-hard
over 12-24 hours. At leather-hard, smooth any rough edges and press the lid in place on the box to check the fit. Trim the lid if it is too tight. Then open the box and allow both pieces to reach
bone-dry
: this may take up to 3 days for a box of this thickness. Seal with two coats of diluted PVA, allowing full drying between coats.
Variations
Impress a pattern into the outside walls with a stamp or lace before assembling.
Add a small clay knob to the lid by scoring and slipping a cylinder of clay onto its surface at centre.
Use paper clay for the walls; the extra tensile strength reduces corner cracking in thinner builds.

items.cause: The walls and base were at different moisture levels when joined, or the interior corners were not blended fully
items.fix: Blend all interior corners thoroughly at joining time. Slow the drying under a cloth for the first day to equalise moisture

items.problem: Lid too tight or too loose
items.cause: The lip strips were positioned too far inward or the lid was not tested at leather-hard
items.fix: At leather-hard, test the lid and shave the strips with a fettling knife if too tight. If loose, add thin coil of clay to the inside edge of the lip

items.cause: Walls attached at angles rather than 90 degrees
items.fix: Check with a square and adjust before the clay stiffens. Using a right-angle corner block as a guide while attaching walls reduces drift
